volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Merchant (Section 1.2.202c - Section 2.2.300c)

sections.label: Section 1.2.202c
sections.translation: Do you e ’ en ask me that ? Why the rogue began to take liberties with her .

sections.label: Section 1.2.203b
sections.translation: Prithee , what , with her ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.204
sections.translation: ’ Twere a wonder if he had taken liberties with myself .

sections.label: Section 1.2.204b
sections.translation: By heavens , my heart is saddened ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.205
sections.original: quod guttatim contabescit , quasi in aquam indideris salem .
sections.translation: which , drop by drop is melting away , just as though you were to put salt in water . I ’ m undone .

sections.label: Section 1.2.206b
sections.original: Em istuc unum verbum dixisti verissimum .
sections.translation: Aye , aye , that one expression have you most truly uttered .

sections.label: Section 1.2.207
sections.translation: This is mere folly .

sections.label: Section 1.2.207b
sections.original: Quid faciam ? credo , non credet pater ,
sections.translation: What shall I do ? I do think my father won ’ t believe me if I say that I bought her for my mother ; and then , besides , it seems to me a shame that I should tell a lie to my parent .

sections.label: Section 1.2.210
sections.original: neque ille credet , neque credibile est forma eximia mulierem ,
sections.translation: He ’ ll neither believe , nor indeed is it credible , that I bought this woman of surpassing beauty as a maid-servant for my mother .

sections.label: Section 1.2.211b
sections.translation: Won ’ t you be quiet , you most silly man ? Troth , he will believe it , for he just now believed me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.212b
sections.translation: I ’ m dreadfully afraid that a suspicion will reach my father how the matter really stands . Prithee , answer me this that I ask you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.214b
sections.translation: What do you ask ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.215
sections.original: Num esse amicam suspicari visus est ?
sections.translation: Did he seem to suspect that she was my mistress ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.215b
sections.translation: He did not seem . On the contrary , in everything , just as I said it , he believed me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.216b
sections.translation: As being true-as he seemed to yourself at least .

sections.label: Section 1.2.217b
sections.translation: Not so ; but he really did believe me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.217c
sections.translation: Ah ! wretched man that I am ! I ’ m ruined ! But why do I kill myself here with repining , and don ’ t be off to the ship ? Follow me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.219b
sections.original: Si istac ibis , commodum obviam venies patri ;
sections.translation: If you go that way , you ’ ll conveniently come slap upon your father .

sections.label: Section 1.2.220
sections.original: postea aspiciet te timidum esse atque exanimatum : ilico
sections.translation: As soon as he shall see you , dismayed and out of spirits , at once he ’ ll be stopping you , and enquiring where you bought her , and for how much you bought her ; he ’ ll be trying you in your dismay .

sections.label: Section 1.2.222b
sections.original: Hac ibo potius . iam censes patrem
sections.translation: I ’ ll go this way in preference . Do you think that by this my father has left the harbour ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.223b
sections.original: Quin ea ego huc praecucurri gratia ,
sections.translation: Why , it was for that reason I ran before him hither , that he mightn ’ t come upon you unawares and fish it out of you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.224b
sections.translation: Very properly done .

sections.label: Section 2.1.225
sections.original: Miris modis di ludos faciunt hominibus
sections.translation: In wondrous ways [*] do the Gods make sport of men , and in wondrous fashions do they send dreams in sleep . As , for instance , I , this very last night that has passed , have sufficiently experienced in my sleep , and , mortal that I am , was much occupied therewith .

sections.label: Section 2.1.230
sections.original: ei ne noceret quam domi ante habui capram
sections.translation: I seemed to have purchased for myself a beautiful she-goat . That she might not offend that other she-goat which I had at home before , and that they mightn ’ t disagree if they were both in the same spot , after that I had purchased her , I seemed to entrust her to the charge of an ape . This ape , not very long afterwards , came to me ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.235
sections.original: male mihi precatur et facit convicium :
sections.translation: uttered imprecations against me , and assailed me with reproaches ; he said that by her means and through the arrival of the she-goat he had suffered injury and loss in no slight degree ; he said that the she-goat , which I had entrusted to him to keep , had gnawed away the marriage-portion of his wife .

sections.label: Section 2.1.240
sections.original: mi illud videri mirum , ut una illaec capra
sections.translation: This seemed extremely wonderful to me , how that this single she-goat could possibly have gnawed away [*] the marriage-portion of the wife of the ape . The ape , however , insisted that it was so , and , in short , gave me this answer , that if I didn ’ t make haste and remove her away from his own house , he would bring her home into my house to my wife .

sections.label: Section 2.1.245
sections.original: atque oppido hercle bene velle illi visus sum ,
sections.translation: And , by my troth , I seemed very greatly to take an interest in her , but not to have any one to whom to entrust this she-goat ; wherefore the more , in my distress , was I tormented with anxiety what to do . Meanwhile , a kid appeared to address me , and began to tell me that he had carried off the she-goat from the ape , and began to laugh at me .

sections.label: Section 2.1.251
sections.original: ego énim lugere atque abductam illam aegre pati .
sections.translation: But I began to lament and complain that she was carried off . To what reality I am to suppose that this vision points , I can ’ t discover ; except that I suspect that I have just now discovered this she-goat , what she is , or what it all means .

sections.label: Section 2.1.255
sections.original: ad portum hinc abii mane cum luci simul ;
sections.translation: This morning , at daybreak , I went away hence down to the harbour . After I had transacted there what I wanted , suddenly I espied the ship from Rhodes , in which my son arrived here yesterday . I had an inclination , I know not why , to visit it ; I went on board a boat , and put off to the ship ;

sections.label: Section 2.1.260
sections.original: atque égo illi aspicio forma eximia mulierem ,
sections.translation: and there I beheld a woman of surpassing beauty , whom my son has brought as a maid-servant for his mother . After I had thus beheld her , I fell in love with her , not as men in their senses , but after the fashion in which madmen are wont . I ’ faith , in former times , in my youthful days , I fell in love ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.265
sections.original: verum ad hoc exemplum numquam , ut nunc insanio .
sections.translation: ’ tis true ; but after this fashion , according as I ’ m now distracted , never . Now beyond a doubt , surely thus this matter stands ; this is that she-goat . But what that ape and that kid mean , I ’ m afraid . One thing , i ’ faith , I really do know for certain , that I ’ m undone for love ; ( to the AUDIENCE ) consider yourselves the other point , what a poor creature I am [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.1.271
sections.original: sed conticiscam , nam eccum it vicinus foras .
sections.translation: But I ’ ll hold my tongue ; lo ! I see my neighbour ; he ’ s coming out of doors .

sections.label: Section 2.2.272
sections.original: Profecto ego illunc hircum castrari volo ,
sections.translation: Really I will have this goat mutilated , that ’ s giving us so much trouble at the farm .

sections.label: Section 2.2.274
sections.original: Nec omen illud mihi nec auspicium placet .
sections.translation: Neither this omen nor this augury pleases me ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.275
sections.original: quasi hircum metuo ne uxor me castret mea .
sections.translation: I ’ m afraid that my wife will be just now mutilating me like the he-goat , and be acting the part of this same ape .

sections.label: Section 2.2.277
sections.original: I tu hinc ad villam atque istos rastros vilico
sections.translation: Do you go hence to my country-house , and take care and deliver personally into his own hands those rakes to the bailiff Pistus himself . Take care and tell my wife that

sections.label: Section 2.2.280
sections.original: mihi esse in urbe , ne me exspectet ; nam mihi
sections.translation: I have business in the city , so that she mayn ’ t expect me ; for do you mention that I have three causes coming on for judgment to-day . Be off , and remember to say this .

sections.label: Section 2.2.282b
sections.translation: Anything more ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.283
sections.translation: That ’ s enough .

sections.label: Section 2.2.283b
sections.translation: Greetings to you , Lysimachus .

sections.label: Section 2.2.283c
sections.translation: Well met ! and greetings to you , Demipho . How are you ? How goes it ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.284b
sections.translation: As with one that ’ s most wretched [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.2.285
sections.translation: May the Gods grant better things .

sections.label: Section 2.2.285b
sections.translation: As for the Gods , it ’ s they that do this .

sections.label: Section 2.2.285c
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.286
sections.original: Dicam , si videam tibi esse óperam aut otium .
sections.translation: I ’ d tell you , if I saw that you had time or leisure .

sections.label: Section 2.2.287
sections.original: Quamquam negotiumst , si quid vis , Demipho ,
sections.translation: Although I have business in hand , if you wish for anything , Demipho , I ’ m never too busy to give attention to a friend .

sections.label: Section 2.2.289
sections.translation: You speak of your kindness to myself who have experienced it . How do I seem to you as to age ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.290b
sections.translation: A subject for Acheron — an antiquated , decrepit old fellow .

sections.label: Section 2.2.291b
sections.translation: You see in a wrong light . I am a child , Lysimachus , of seven years old .

sections.label: Section 2.2.292b
sections.translation: Are you in your senses , to say that you are a child ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.293b
sections.translation: I ’ m telling what ’ s true .

sections.label: Section 2.2.294
sections.original: Modo hercle in mentem venit , quid tu diceres :
sections.translation: I ’ faith , it has this moment come into my mind what you mean to say ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.295
sections.original: senex quom extemplo est , iam nec sentit nec sapit ,
sections.translation: directly a person is old , no longer has he sense or taste ; people say that he has become a child again .

sections.label: Section 2.2.297
sections.original: Immo bis tanto valeo quam valui prius .
sections.translation: Why , no ; for I ’ m twice as hearty as ever I was before .

sections.label: Section 2.2.298
sections.translation: I ’ faith , it ’ s well that so it is , and I ’ m glad of it .

sections.label: Section 2.2.298b
sections.translation: Aye , and if you did but know ; with my eyes , too , I see even better now than I did formerly .

sections.label: Section 2.2.300
sections.translation: That ’ s good .

sections.label: Section 2.2.300b
sections.translation: Of a thing that ’ s bad , I ’ m speaking .

sections.label: Section 2.2.300c
sections.translation: Then that same is not good .
